beats.narration_text: Fire a fast particle at a potential. It deflects forward a little, or sideways more, or straight back. Classical physics: the angle tracks the force. Quantum physics: each angle reads a different spatial frequency.
The Born approximation says the scattering pattern is the Fourier transform of the potential. Every deflection angle is one frequency component of the force.
Why does a scattering angle probe a Fourier component — and how does a detector reconstruct the shape of a force?

props.headline: Why does a scattering angle probe a Fourier component — and…

card.headline: Each angle measures one Fourier mode of the potential.
card.dek: The Born approximation: scattering as diffraction on the force

beats.narration_text: When a particle deflects by angle theta, its momentum changes by q equals 2 k times sine of theta over 2. Small angle: small momentum transfer. Large angle: large momentum transfer.
The Born amplitude is the Fourier transform of the potential evaluated at that momentum transfer q. Small q probes long-range structure. Large q probes fine structure.
Forward scattering at q=0 reads the volume integral of V — its total strength. Backscattering at q=2k reads the highest available spatial frequency — the potential's finest details.
Scan your detector through all angles and you build up the full Fourier spectrum. That spectrum is the potential itself. Scattering is diffraction on the force.
Extended targets have a form factor — the Fourier transform of the charge distribution. Large-angle scattering is suppressed because the extended target has no high-frequency structure.
This is how SLAC found quarks in 1969. Deep inelastic scattering showed structure at large q where a smooth proton would show none. The hard substructure scattered at large angles.

Illustrative: Yukawa potential, range 1 over mu equals 1 fm. At ka equals 5, forward peak is narrow — only q near 0 is large. At theta equals 90 degrees, q equals k-root-2 and the cross-section falls by the form factor squared.
The detector is a Fourier analyzer. Each angle reads one spatial frequency of the force. That is the Born approximation.

card.headline: f_Born(θ) = −(m/2πℏ²) Ṽ(q)
card.dek: q = 2k sin(θ/2): every angle probes a different Fourier mode of the potential.
